raw_case:
Arthroscopic shoulder surgery for gouty long head of biceps tendinitis: a case report


Objective

The objective of the case report is to analyze the clinical manifestations and imaging features of gouty long head of biceps tendinitis, and to summarize the methods and effects of shoulder arthroscopic surgery.

Clinical presentation and intervention

The clinical data of a 39-year-old Han Chinese female with gouty long head of the biceps tendinitis was retrospectively analyzed, and the clinical manifestations, imaging features, and diagnosis and treatment were analyzed. The patient presented with pain and limited movement of right shoulder joint. Computed tomography showed irregular high-density shadows above the glenoid and adjacent to the coracoid process of the right shoulder. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed superior labrum anterior and posterior injury with edema in the upper recess and axillary sac. After arthroscopic surgery, the “tofu residue” tissue of the long head of the biceps was removed, and the postoperative pathological examination proved that it was gout stone.

Conclusion

Gouty long head of the biceps tendinitis is a rare disease. Arthroscopic surgery can probe the structural lesions of shoulder cavity in all aspects, improve the surgical accuracy, and reduce the trauma.

Introduction

Long head of biceps tendinitis is a common orthopedic injury. It is mainly caused by repeated friction of the long head of biceps tendon in the humerus intertubercular sulcus, resulting in local inflammation, degeneration, and adhesion of the tendon sheath, which eventually lead to serious physiological dysfunction of the tendon. The clinical manifestations of the patient are pain and discomfort in front of the shoulder, limited mobility of the shoulder joint, and so on, which can cause serious impact on daily life and work. Gout is a metabolic disease caused by long-term purine metabolism disorders. Gout stones usually deposit in joints, tissues around joints, and under the skin. Gouty bicipital long head tendinitis is rare in clinic, usually caused by gout stones deposited in the glenoid tubercle of the long head tendon. The clinical data of a case of gouty long head of biceps tendinitis treated by arthroscopic operation were retrospectively analyzed to provide reference for clinical treatment of this type of patient.

Case report

The patient, a 39-year-old Han Chinese female, was admitted to the Orthopedics and Traumatology Department of Wujiang District Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine in Suzhou City for 1 week due to right shoulder joint pain and limited movement for 18 months. The patient presented with front pain of right shoulder joint without obvious inducement 18 months ago that was sometimes good or bad and was occasionally accompanied by dysfunction, with no local redness, swelling, and fever. The pain suddenly worsened after heavy weight 1 week prior to presentation, and she came to the hospital for treatment. Upon physical examination, we found acute pain appearance, no swelling of the right shoulder joint, no “square shoulder” or other deformity, obvious tenderness of the upper front of the shoulder joint, obvious restriction of active shoulder movement, increased pain on passive activity, active forward bending of the right shoulder joint about 70°, internal rotation of the thumb to L4, external rotation of 15°, and abduction of 45°. The right shoulder joint passively extended about 80°, the thumb internally rotated to L3, the thumb externally rotated 20°, and the abduction was 60°. Job’s sign (+), Neer’s sign (+), Belleypush sign (+), O’Brein sign (+), Haukin’s sign (−) and Speed (+), right peripheral blood flow, sensation, and movement were normal, and the patient was previous healthly. After admission, computed tomography (CT) scan of the right shoulder joint showed an irregular high-density shadow above the glenoid and adjacent to the coracoid process of the right shoulder, indicating possible avulsion fracture (Fig. 1). The MRI of the right shoulder joint suggested a superior labrum anterior and posterior (SLAP) injury, excessive fluid accumulation in the glenohumeral joint space, and edema in the upper recess and axillary sac (Fig. 2A–C). X-ray images of the right shoulder joint showed that a high-density shadow (0.5 × 1.0 cm) was visible above the glenoid of the right shoulder joint (Fig. 2D). Laboratory examination showed that the white blood cell count was 10.14 × 109/L. The neutrophil percentage was 88.21%, lymphocyte percentage was 7.9%, and blood uric acid was 397 μmol/L (normal value: 120–-20), and the rest of the test results were normal. Preoperative diagnosis was a superior labrum anterior and posterior (SLAP) injury of the right shoulder glenoid margin and avulsion fracture of the right shoulder glenoid to be arranged.All preoperative examinations were completed, no contraindications were confirmed, and right shoulder arthroscopic exploration was performed after full communication with the patient. Extensive hyperemia was observed in the synovial cavity of the right shoulder joint during the operation. In addition, white calcium-like material deposits were observed on the synovial and glenohumeral articular cartilaginous surfaces. It was found that there was an accumulation of white lime powder that was 0.5 cm × 0.5 cm in size at the attachment of the upper recess and the insertion point of the biceps long tendon (Fig. 3a). After planing and cleaning, a tear of about 0.3 cm was found at the junction of the upper recess and the insertion point of the biceps long tendon. Planing was repeated, and it was washed repeatedly until all the white material was removed. A plasma knife head was used for electric coagulation and contraction of the breach. The glenoid lip at the attachment point of the long head tendon of the biceps was checked to be stable without obvious relaxation, and other tissue structures were investigated without abnormality. After the subacromial space was examined, no obvious white crystal deposition was found on the subacromial structure, deltoid bursae, and other synovial surfaces. Finally, the joint cavity was repeatedly rinsed to finish the operation. After operation, the normal scan of the right shoulder joint found that the irregular high-density shadow above the right shoulder glenoid disappeared (Fig. 3C). A focal tissue section [hematoxylin and eosin (HE staining)] showed gout stones (Fig. 3D), which ruled out other diseases and supported the diagnosis of gouty shoulder arthritis.After the operation, the pain of the right shoulder joint was significantly relieved. The patient was instructed to perform functional exercise of the right shoulder joint and was given oral allopurinol (Beijing Yanjing Pharmaceutical Co., LTD., H11020432; 50 mg once or twice a day; treatment for 1 month), sodium bicarbonate [Yuanda Pharmaceutical (China) Co., LTD., Sinopath H42021753, 1 tablet at a time, 3 times a day oral, treatment for 1 month] comprehensive uric-acid-lowering treatment. At 2 months after surgery, the patient’s right shoulder pain disappeared completely, and the range of motion returned to normal. Plain scan reconstruction of the right shoulder joint revealed that the irregular high-density shadow previously visible above the glenoid had disappeared, further confirming the effectiveness of surgical treatment.

Discussion

Gout is a typical metabolic disease that results in abnormally high uric acid levels in the body due to a long-term disorder or disorder of purine metabolism. When the concentration of uric acid exceeds its saturation point in the blood, urate will crystallize and deposit in joints, surrounding soft tissues, and subcutaneously, causing a series of pathological changes [1–4]. Gout not only destroys the joints but also may trigger nephritis, urinary calculi, and cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases, and sometimes even cause systemic inflammation. Gout stones, a typical pathological feature of gout, usually form in the subcutaneous tissue near the joint capsule or at the end of the limb. These turgout stones are essentially composed of foreign body granulomas and urate crystals of different sizes, neutrophils, giant cells, epithelioid cells, and fibrous tissues [5, 6]. It is easy to cause damage to the joint cartilage, which can lead to joint pain and dysfunction. For joint damage caused by gouty stone, it is often difficult to achieve satisfactory results with simple conservative treatment. Studies have shown that surgical treatment of gout and its associated joint problems can achieve better curative effect [7, 8].SLAP refers to the anterior and posterior tears of the upper lip of the glenoid margin, affecting the attachment point of the long head tendon of the biceps muscle. SLAP is one of the causes of chronic shoulder pain or shoulder instability. However, the injury mechanism of SLAP is complex and variable, and the clinical manifestations lack specificity. Currently, diagnosis of SLAP faces many challenges, and imaging studies are limited due to insufficient sensitivity. MRI is helpful not only in diagnosing SLAP injury but also in identifying other possible shoulder lesions, such as rotator cuff tears, calcifying myositis, or scapulohumeral periarthritis. However, MRI diagnosis of SLAP injury has a certain false positive rate, especially due to the possible presence of normal anatomical variations similar to SLAP injury in the upper pelvic lip [9, 10]. Arthroscopy is the “gold standard” for diagnosing SLAP injuries, but it is somewhat invasive and cannot be used as a routine examination. As a result, diagnosis of SLAP is difficult in orthopedic research, and it is easily confused with other joint diseases.In this case, the patient was a young woman with intermittent pain in the right shoulder joint for nearly a year and a half, and no discomfort in other joints. Based on the imaging and clinical findings, the initial consideration was a SLAP injury in the right shoulder. However, during surgery, it was found that there were large amounts of white crystals deposited in the joint cavity on the long head of the biceps tendon insertion, synovial membrane, and cartilage surface, which ruled out the possibility of SLAP injury. Combined with the pathological examination results after surgery, the final diagnosis was gouty right shoulder long head of biceps tendinitis. Therefore, even if the blood uric acid test results are negative during clinical diagnosis and treatment, the possibility of gouty arthritis cannot be completely ruled out [11]. It should be combined with clinical manifestations, imaging examination, necessary surgical exploration results, and even postoperative pathological examination to determine the diagnosis and timely treatment. We used shoulder arthroscopic surgery to treat this case, and the results showed that the operation could successfully remove the gouty stone that affected the joint function, and the postoperative drug treatment could effectively control the uric acid level in the patient, thus reducing the joint symptoms and restoring the joint function. It is suggested that arthroscopic exploration can be used for the diagnosis of shoulder joint diseases with unclear diagnosis. For gouty arthritis patients, when conservative treatment is not good, surgical removal of gouty stone can be considered to improve the treatment effect.However, the follow-up period for this study was slightly shorter. Mainly because the patient felt that the postoperative effect was very good, pain completely disappeared, and the joint motion returned to normal, she was not willing to come to the hospital for follow-up consultation. This is indeed a limitation of our study, which might have been more perfect if the follow-up period had been longer.

Conclusion

Gouty long head of biceps tendinitis causes shoulder joint pain and limited movement. The symptoms are similar to those of other shoulder diseases, and it is easy for it to be misdiagnosed or even missed. For these diseases, arthroscopic exploration should be considered when there is no obvious effect after routine diagnosis and treatment. Arthroscopic technology not only facilitates accurate diagnosis but also provides minimally invasive treatment options that facilitate rapid recovery.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 39-year-old Han Chinese female.
- **Chief Complaint:** Right shoulder pain and limited movement.
- **History of Present Illness:** Intermittent front-right shoulder pain for 18 months, worsened after lifting a heavy weight one week before presentation. No local redness, swelling, or fever.
- **Past Medical History:** Previously healthy.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Acute pain; limited active and passive motion of the right shoulder. Findings include tenderness at the upper front shoulder joint, Job’s sign (+), Neer’s sign (+), Belleypush sign (+), O’Brein sign (+), Haukin’s sign (−), Speed (+). No significant deformities, with an absence of redness, swelling, or fever. Peripheral flow, sensation, and movement were normal.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Imaging: CT showed irregular high-density shadow above the glenoid and adjacent to the coracoid process. MRI revealed superior labrum anterior and posterior (SLAP) lesion with fluid accumulation and edema. X-ray showed a high-density lesion above the right glenoid.
  - Labs: Elevated blood uric acid (397 µmol/L), elevated neutrophil percentage (88.21%), and normal lymphocyte percentage (7.9%). White blood cell count was normal at 10.14 × 10^9/L.
  - Postoperative Pathology: Tissue analysis confirmed gout stone.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential Diagnoses:** Based on clinical history and imaging findings, the primary consideration was a SLAP injury of the right shoulder. Other possibilities included gouty arthritis, calcifying tendinitis, or nonspecific arthritis.
2. **SLAP Injury Consideration:** While imaging supported a SLAP injury, arthroscopic findings contradicted this with the presence of large white crystal deposits, suggesting an alternative etiology. Eliminated as primary cause after surgical exploration.
3. **Gouty Arthritis Consideration:** The deposits, along with postoperative pathological confirmation, supported a diagnosis of gout. Elevated uric acid levels strengthened this suspicion, despite the limitation of being a single joint involvement (rare for gout). Blood test results and imaging suggested gouty arthritis.
4. **Calcifying Tendinitis:** Ruled out due to pathological confirmation of gout stones and lack of calcium hydroxyapatite crystal features.
5. **Nonspecific Arthritis:** Excluded based on the specific findings of gout crystal deposits and tissue pathology results confirming gout stones.

The diagnosis of gouty long head of biceps tendinitis was made given the pathological findings during surgery and imaging features consistent with gouty stone deposits.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Gouty long head of biceps tendinitis.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Gouty long head of biceps tendinitis.

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- **Patient Information:** 39-year-old Han Chinese female.
- **Chief Complaint:** Right shoulder pain and limited movement.
- **History of Present Illness:** Intermittent front-right shoulder pain for 18 months, worsened after lifting a heavy weight one week before presentation. No local redness, swelling, or fever.
- **Past Medical History:** Previously healthy.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Acute pain; limited active and passive motion of the right shoulder. Findings include tenderness at the upper front shoulder joint, Job’s sign (+), Neer’s sign (+), Belleypush sign (+), O’Brein sign (+), Haukin’s sign (−), Speed (+). No significant deformities, with an absence of redness, swelling, or fever. Peripheral flow, sensation, and movement were normal.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Imaging: CT showed irregular high-density shadow above the glenoid and adjacent to the coracoid process. MRI revealed superior labrum anterior and posterior (SLAP) lesion with fluid accumulation and edema. X-ray showed a high-density lesion above the right glenoid.
  - Labs: Elevated blood uric acid (397 µmol/L), elevated neutrophil percentage (88.21%), and normal lymphocyte percentage (7.9%). White blood cell count was normal at 10.14 × 10^9/L.
  - Postoperative Pathology: Tissue analysis confirmed gout stone.

---

**Format to Follow:**

```

<step 1> Specific thinking content of this step
<step 2> Specific thinking content of this step
...
<step n> Specific thinking content of this step

[Just output the diagnostic result without any other explanation.]
```
results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 39-year-old Han Chinese woman with right shoulder pain and limited movement. She's had intermittent pain for 18 months, which got worse after lifting a heavy weight. No redness, swelling, or fever, which is important because that might rule out some infections or inflammatory conditions.

Looking at the physical exam, she has tenderness at the upper front shoulder, and several positive signs like Job’s, Neer’s, Belleypush, O’Brein, and Speed. These signs are usually associated with rotator cuff issues or labral injuries. Haukin’s is negative, which might be less relevant here. The active and passive motion is limited, pointing to a structural problem rather than just muscle strain.

Imaging shows a high-density shadow above the glenoid on CT and X-ray, and MRI reveals a SLAP lesion with fluid and edema. The SLAP lesion is a tear of the superior labrum, which can cause shoulder pain and limited motion. But the presence of a high-density lesion might suggest something else, like calcification or a bone spur. However, the postoperative pathology found gout stones. That's key. Gout typically presents with uric acid crystals in joints, but the shoulder isn't a common site. However, the elevated uric acid level (397 µmol/L) is definitely above the normal range, which is a clue.

The labs show elevated neutrophils and uric acid. Neutrophils are part of the immune response, but without infection signs (no fever, redness), maybe the elevated neutrophils are due to the gout flare-up. Gout can cause acute inflammation, but the chronic nature of the pain (18 months) is a bit confusing. Usually, gout is more acute, but chronic gouty arthritis can develop if not managed. The fact that the pathology confirmed gout stones suggests that the high-density shadow is actually tophi or uric acid deposits in the shoulder area.

So putting it all together: the SLAP lesion might be a secondary finding due to the gout causing mechanical issues. The shoulder pain and limited movement could be from both the labral tear and the gout deposits. But the key is that the pathology confirmed gout, so the primary diagnosis is gouty arthritis in the shoulder, with a concurrent SLAP lesion. The elevated uric acid is the main indicator here. The other signs and imaging findings support the presence of a structural issue, but the pathology result points to gout as the underlying cause.
</think>

```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with chronic right shoulder pain exacerbated by heavy lifting, with physical findings suggestive of rotator cuff or labral pathology (positive Job’s, Neer’s, and Speed signs). However, the absence of inflammatory signs (no redness, swelling, fever) and the presence of elevated uric acid (397 µmol/L) raise suspicion for gout as a potential underlying cause.  
<step 2> Imaging reveals a high-density lesion above the glenoid, which could represent calcification, bone spurs, or uric acid deposits. MRI findings of a SLAP lesion with edema suggest mechanical shoulder pathology, but the postoperative pathology confirming gout stones directly links the lesion to gouty deposits.  
<step 3> Elevated neutrophils (88.21%) may reflect an inflammatory response to uric acid crystal deposition, even in the absence of acute infection. The chronic nature of the pain (18 months) aligns with chronic gouty arthritis, which can cause joint damage and secondary structural issues like labral tears.  
<step 4> The combination of elevated uric acid, imaging findings of a high-density lesion, and pathological confirmation of gout stones solidifies the diagnosis of gouty arthritis in the shoulder, with a concurrent SLAP lesion likely secondary to chronic inflammation and mechanical stress.  

### Answer:
Gouty arthritis of the right shoulder with concurrent superior labrum anterior and posterior (SLAP) lesion.
